main_summary.summary: Joint oral history interview with Simeon Booker and Moses Newson, two Black journalists who covered the Civil Rights Movement from its mid-1950s inception through the late 1960s. Conducted by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program on July 13, 2011, in Washington, D.C., for the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture. Booker entered journalism with the Baltimore Afro-American in 1942, moved to the Cleveland Call & Post, received a Nieman Fellowship at Harvard, then joined John H. Johnson at Jet magazine, where he eventually ran the Washington bureau. Newson came out of the Navy, attended Storer College and Lincoln University's School of Journalism, and joined the Tri-State Defender in Memphis in 1952. Both covered the 1955 Emmett Till murder and trial in Mississippi, the 1957 Little Rock school desegregation crisis (including the mob beating of L. Alex Wilson), and the 1961 Freedom Rides. Newson was aboard the Greyhound bus firebombed by a mob outside Anniston, Alabama; Booker was on the trailing Trailways bus and escaped the Birmingham mob by hailing a Black cab to Rev. Shuttlesworth's home. They discuss reporting strategies, the Black-press stringer system, segregated press tables in Mississippi courthouses, and the impact of television coverage on the Movement's progress.
key_themes: Black press journalism during the Civil Rights Movement
Emmett Till murder and trial coverage
Little Rock school desegregation and L. Alex Wilson beating
Freedom Rides and the Anniston bus burning
Press strategy in segregated Southern communities
main_summary.historical_significance:
This interview is a rare joint primary-source account from two Black journalists who were eyewitnesses to multiple foundational events of the Civil Rights Movement. Newson's first-person narration of the May 14, 1961 firebombing of the Greyhound bus outside Anniston, Alabama, including the role of state trooper E.L. Cowling in saving the riders' lives, is a direct eyewitness account of one of the most photographed moments of the Freedom Rides. Booker's account of the parallel Trailways bus arrival in Birmingham and his escape via a Black cab driver to Rev. Fred Shuttlesworth's home is similarly first-person testimony.

Both journalists provide ground-level documentation of the conditions under which the Black press operated: segregated press tables in the Tallahatchie County courthouse during the Emmett Till trial, the stringer system used by Jet to develop sources in rural areas, the necessity of changing clothes to search for witnesses on Mississippi Delta plantations, and the role of Medgar Evers as driver for NAACP witness-finding teams. The interview also preserves Booker's recollection of his last extended interview with Dr. King during the Poor People's Campaign planning, including King's response to questions about whether he was failing.

chapters.title: Entry into Journalism
chapters.summary: Booker traces his career from the Baltimore Afro-American in 1942 through the Cleveland Call & Post, a Nieman Fellowship at Harvard, and the founding of Jet magazine's Washington bureau under publisher John H. Johnson. Newson describes his Navy service, undergraduate years at Storer College in West Virginia, journalism studies at Lincoln University in Missouri, and his 1952 start at the Tri-State Defender in Memphis.
related_events: John H. Johnson's founding of Jet

chapters.title: Hoxie and Clinton School Desegregation
chapters.summary: Newson recalls covering early school desegregation efforts in Hoxie, Arkansas (1955), where media were unusually welcomed into classrooms, and in Clinton, Tennessee (1956), where outside agitators including John Kasper and the KKK fired up white residents against the small Black community on Foley Hill. National Guard intervention was eventually required.

chapters.title: Emmett Till Case
chapters.summary: Booker describes Jet's coverage of the September 1955 murder of Emmett Till in Money, Mississippi, including David Jackson's open-casket funeral photographs at Mamie Till's insistence. Newson recounts the trial in Tallahatchie County, including the segregated press table and his work with NAACP teams and driver Medgar Evers to find Black witnesses on Delta plantations.

chapters.title: Black Press Reporting Strategies
chapters.summary: Both journalists describe how Black newspapers built coverage networks: Jet's nationwide stringer system that turned rural readers into informants and local guides, the necessity of changing clothes to interview witnesses on Mississippi plantations, segregated courthouses, and the constant pressure on Black sources whose livelihoods were tied to white landowners.
related_events: Charles C. Diggs Jr observer at Till trial

chapters.title: Little Rock 1957
chapters.summary: Newson describes covering the Little Rock Nine, including being ordered out of the area by a National Guard colonel, the September 1957 mob attack on Black reporters including L. Alex Wilson (who refused to run after a prior KKK incident in Leesburg, Florida), and James L. Hicks of the Amsterdam News. Both discuss Elizabeth Eckford's isolated walk to Central High and President Eisenhower's decision to send in federal troops.
related_events: Little Rock Central High desegregation September 1957

chapters.title: Race Relations and the Early 1960s
chapters.summary: The journalists assess the political climate around Kennedy's 1960 election, contrasting Eisenhower's reluctance to speak about civil rights with Kennedy's openness to the press. Newson describes a Mississippi church meeting where a sharecropper, evicted for trying to register, asked the audience to keep registering rather than help him. The chapter touches on the Harry and Harriette Moore Christmas 1951 bombing in Florida.

chapters.title: Freedom Rides 1961
chapters.summary: Newson describes the May 14, 1961 firebombing of the Greyhound bus outside Anniston, Alabama, including state trooper E.L. Cowling drawing his pistol to keep the mob off the bus, and the legal foundation laid by CORE's 1947 ride, the 1946 Morgan v. Virginia case, the four-women interstate-travel case argued by Attorney Fred Gray, and Boynton v. Virginia. Booker recounts arriving in Birmingham on the Trailways bus, ducking out the back, and being driven by a Black cab driver to Rev. Shuttlesworth, who organized a rescue caravan.

chapters.title: March on Washington and King
chapters.summary: Newson describes attending the 1963 March on Washington and the coalition of labor and church leaders. Booker recalls his final extended interview with Dr. King during Poor People's Campaign planning, conducted in a car en route to the airport with Andrew Young, Harry Belafonte, and Coretta Scott King present. King's response to charges of failure: 'if I'm failing, then... Jesus Christ failed because He got himself hung on the cross.'

chapters.title: Black Press and White Press Relations
chapters.summary: Both journalists reflect on the divide between Black and white newspapers during the Movement years. Newson describes working as the only Black reporter at major events, unable to stay at the same hotels as white colleagues, but maintaining professional working relationships. Booker covered multiple Republican and Democratic conventions; Newson did not cover the 1964 Atlantic City convention.
related_events: Republican conventions Miami New York Kansas City

chapters.title: Television and the Movement
chapters.summary: Booker argues that white press involvement, particularly television networks broadcasting images of police dogs, fire hoses, and beatings, accelerated the Movement in ways the Black press alone could not. He raises critical questions about contemporary mainstream coverage, citing the 2009 Henry Louis Gates Jr. arrest as an example of incomplete reporting.

chapters.title: Robert Kennedy and the Late 1960s
chapters.summary: Booker reflects on Robert Kennedy as the first federal official genuinely responsive to racial matters, his 1968 assassination, and the political shift under Lyndon Johnson. The interview closes with Booker's assessment that the political coalition disintegrated after 1968 and was only partially rebuilt through Obama's election.

loc_video.caption: Simeon Booker and Moses James Newson oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Washington, D.C., 2011 July 13
